primary_diagnosis.name: Large local reaction to insect bite

pathophysiology_summary: A large local reaction to an arthropod bite is an IgE/cell-mediated hypersensitivity to injected saliva, producing a rapidly developing (hours), firm, indurated, intensely pruritic wheal/papule with a central punctum and surrounding erythema; it is benign and self-limiting over hours to a few days. The ground-truth tasks are to distinguish it from (1) systemic anaphylaxis (airway/CVS/GI features — absent here), (2) early cellulitis/lymphangitis (slower onset over days, increasingly PAINFUL rather than itchy, spreading erythema ± red streaking, fever), and (3) tick-borne illness (embedded tick, or a slowly expanding target/erythema-migrans rash). Management is a non-sedating antihistamine, a topical corticosteroid, cold compresses and not scratching (to avoid secondary infection).

differential.diagnosis: Large local reaction to insect bite
differential.evidence_basis: Rapid firm itchy wheal with a central punctum after gardening.
Screen airway/CVS/GI features — a bite can trigger anaphylaxis.
Screen for slower painful spreading erythema ± streaking/fever.
Consider given outdoor exposure; no embedded tick or slowly expanding target rash here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Local reaction leads; anaphylaxis and cellulitis must be actively excluded before topical management.

differential.diagnosis: Large local reaction to insect bite
differential.evidence_basis: No systemic anaphylaxis, rapid itch-predominant onset with central punctum, no streaking/fever — benign large local reaction.
No airway/systemic features; escalation target if they develop.
No painful spreading erythema/streaking/fever; escalation target over the next days if it develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative anaphylaxis/cellulitis screen, a large local reaction leads; antihistamine + topical steroid, with anaphylaxis and cellulitis safety-nets.

differential.diagnosis: Large local reaction to insect bite
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting over hours to days.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a benign large local reaction.

red_flags.description: Anaphylaxis — breathing difficulty, lip/tongue/throat swelling, generalised urticaria, dizziness/faintness
Cellulitis/lymphangitis — increasingly painful spreading erythema, red streak up the limb, fever/rigors (over days)

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-diagnosing cellulitis on a fresh itchy bite
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A hot itchy wheal within hours is a local reaction, not cellulitis (which is slower and painful) — avoid unnecessary antibiotics.
Not screening airway/systemic features on an acute bite reaction.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for fluctuance/induration extent and regional nodes
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Antihistamine + topical corticosteroid + cold compress; 000 for anaphylaxis; GP/ED if cellulitis develops over days.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing anaphylaxis, or a later secondary cellulitis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Insect bites and stings
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Local reactions: oral antihistamine, topical corticosteroid, cold compresses, avoid scratching. Watch for anaphylaxis (adrenaline) and secondary cellulitis (antibiotics). Remove any tick appropriately.
